narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam based on academic and personal profiles like yours. The model estimated a 90% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which directly led to the rejection of your admission because the school prioritizes candidates with stronger predicted odds of passing. This high failure risk stems primarily from three key factors in your profile that most strongly shaped the model's decision: your Year 3 school decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 school decile ranking. The most influential was your Year 3 decile of 1, which is in the bottom tier compared to the typical student's midpoint around 5.5; this low ranking signaled to the model a significant push toward predicting bar failure, as it reflects challenges in sustaining academic performance later in school. Next, your LSAT score of 19.3—well below the average of 36.7—further reinforced this risk, strongly contributing to the failure prediction by highlighting a gap in standardized test readiness essential for bar success. Your Year 1 decile of 1, also at the bottom compared to the average around 5.7, added to the concern, though with somewhat less weight, indicating early academic struggles that the model sees as a continued hurdle. While your plan to attend full-time aligns with the vast majority of students (over 90%), it didn't offset these core academic red flags. In contrast to typical admitted students, who often have mid-to-high deciles and LSAT scores closer to or above 36, your metrics placed you in the lower range, amplifying the model's caution. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment to help the school maintain high bar passage rates. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your undergraduate record if possible through post-bac courses, or targeting schools with more flexible admissions while building a standout personal statement that showcases your resilience. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early or explore part-time programs if that better fits your circumstances. With focused improvements, you can reshape your profile for future applications.
